title: Decomposed Tasks

content.question: What goal feels stuck because the next step isn't small enough?
sop: State the goal clearly, in one sentence
Break it down until the next step takes less than 15 minutes
Rate the current task's size and clarity before you start (see calculation, page 5)
Do that one small step, nothing else on the list matters until it's done
Repeat: define the next tiny step only once the last one is finished
content.explainer: 'I don't know where to start' is almost never a motivation problem, it's a sizing problem. A goal stated at the scale of 'grow the business' has no obvious next physical action, which is exactly why it stalls; the same goal broken down to 'write one sentence' has an obvious one, which is why it moves.
questions: What goal feels stuck because the next step isn't small enough?
What project have you been avoiding for weeks that you've never actually broken down into a first physical action?
If your next step took less than 10 minutes, would you still be procrastinating on it?
content.equation_example: Task Size: rate 1-10 (10 = huge, vague, multi-week). At Task Size 9, Clarity is proportional to 1 divided by 9, approximately 0.11, you barely know where to start. Break it into a step sized 2: Clarity is proportional to 1 divided by 2 = 0.5, obvious what to do next. Smaller task size (a falling number) means rising clarity; a large, undefined task size means low clarity and stalling.
content.closing_statement: Stuck usually means the next step is still too big.

caption:
Principle Nº 231: Decomposed Tasks

Break projects into micro-actions.

Ask yourself: What goal feels stuck because the next step isn't small enough?
